He also discussed significant organizational changes, stating, "We began executing on a set of significant organizational changes, work that's continued into quarter 1 that we believe will strengthen leadership, enhance growth and improve efficiencies."
  • Warwick announced the integration of Trade Publishing, Book Fairs, and Book Clubs into a new Children's Book group under Sasha Quinton's leadership, with Jackie De Leo joining to further strengthen the team. He described this as a move to "unlock further efficiencies and potential in our vertically integrated children's book publishing and distribution."
  • Haji Glover, CFO, reported, "Revenue increased 7% to $508.3 million in the fourth quarter and was up 2% to $1,625.5 million for the fiscal year." He added, "Adjusted operating income decreased to $63.4 million in the fourth quarter from $66.8 million in the prior year period." Glover noted that lower adjusted operating income was primarily due to amortization expenses from the 9 Story acquisition. He also stated, "Adjusted EBITDA increased 1% to $91.2 million in the fourth quarter and was up 6% to $145.4 million for the fiscal year."

Outlook

  • Scholastic is targeting adjusted EBITDA of $160 million to $170 million for fiscal 2026, with Warwick stating, "At the midpoint of guidance, adjusted EBITDA is expected to grow 20%, excluding $10 million in incremental tariff expense currently anticipated from implemented or announced tariffs."
  • Fiscal 2026 revenue is expected to grow 2% to 4%. Glover explained that this reflects "strength in our core businesses, partially offset by continuing headwinds on consumer spending."
  • The company expects free cash flow in fiscal 2026 of $30 million to $40 million, attributing this to "higher expected earnings, improved working capital and lower cash tax, partially offset by higher capital investments and other accrued expenses."
  • Glover detailed that cost-saving actions taken are expected to result in $10 million of additional benefit in fiscal 2026, with incremental $15 million to $20 million in further cost savings planned.

Financial Results

  • In the Children's Book Publishing and Distribution segment, fourth quarter revenue increased 9% to $288.2 million. Trade Publishing revenue rose 19% to $97.3 million, driven by the new Hunger Games title. Book Fairs revenue increased 5% to $177.8 million, while Book Clubs revenue was $13.1 million in the quarter.
  • Education segment revenue was $125.7 million, down 7% from the prior year period.
  • Entertainment segment revenue was $14.8 million compared to $0.6 million in the prior year period, reflecting the 9 Story Media Group acquisition.
  • International segment revenue increased 8% to $76.8 million.
  • Free cash flow was $29.2 million for fiscal 2025, and net debt at year-end was $136.6 million, primarily reflecting the 9 Story acquisition and capital returned to shareholders.
  • The company returned over $92 million to shareholders in fiscal 2025, including nearly 3.5 million shares repurchased.

Q&A

  • Brendan Michael McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC: Asked about sources of cost savings for fiscal '26. Haji Glover: "A majority of the cost actions are coming out of non -- more discretionary functions, things that we can cut back on as we're looking to be more frugal as an organization."
  • McCarthy: Asked if overhead would decrease in fiscal '26. Glover: "We're getting the full year impact of the stuff right in FY '26. So the FY '25 stuff as we showed in -- we talked about in the script, we're experiencing -- we saw $15 million of that in FY '25, and we're going to see another $10 million in FY '26."
  • McCarthy: Questioned expectations for flat revenue in Education. Warwick: "The market continues to be cyclically difficult, especially with districts and schools but we do expect the situation to progressively improve just because of the normal cyclicality of the education business."
  • McCarthy: Asked about the pipeline for state-sponsored programs. Warwick: "I'm pretty optimistic that things are going well, both in terms of being able to expand what we do in those states... we have multiple conversations which are going on with state governments."
  • McCarthy: Inquired about internal feedback from combining trade and school channels. Warwick: "The main impact at the moment is really internal rather than external. And it's been tremendous, actually... we have the opportunity to be able to link what we're selling in schools through the book fairs and book club channels in a much more integrated way than we've done before with the Trade Publishing."
  • McCarthy: Asked about entertainment profitability. Glover: "We expect it to be slightly lower, but in line with this year."
  • McCarthy: Requested timing for real estate monetization and capital allocation priorities. Glover: "We're hoping, as I mentioned earlier, to have something within the next 90 to 120 days. In terms of our capital allocation priorities, we're going to remain consistent with returning capital to shareholders as possible when we can."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions centered on cost savings, education segment outlook, and real estate monetization, with a neutral tone and requests for clarity rather than pressing skepticism.
  • Management maintained a confident and forward-looking tone during prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing successful cost management, growth initiatives, and optimism about future performance. Warwick concluded, "I'm excited about the new fiscal year ahead. I mean, I think we're in good momentum. We've got a positive outlook, and we continue to focus on creating shareholder value and impact, that's so important to us."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management sentiment remained steady, with management slightly more confident due to recent organizational changes and strong year-end financials.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for adjusted EBITDA increased from a previous forecast of approximately $140 million for fiscal 2025 to a new range of $160 million to $170 million for fiscal 2026, targeting 20% growth at the midpoint.
  • Revenue growth expectations remain modest, with continued pressure on education and consumer segments. Strategic focus has shifted toward integrating business units and implementing cost-saving programs.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from concerns about macroeconomic headwinds and funding uncertainty in education to more detailed questions on cost actions and capital allocation.
  • Management's confidence increased as they highlighted tangible cost reductions, integration benefits, and forward progress on capital structure optimization.
  • The company advanced its real estate monetization strategy, moving from disclosure of potential asset values to active engagement with Newmark Group for sale-leaseback transactions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing headwinds in consumer and school spending, with the education segment experiencing revenue decline due to a pressured supplemental curriculum market.
  • Incremental tariff expenses of $10 million are anticipated for fiscal 2026, primarily impacting nonbook and novelty items.
  • Entertainment segment profitability faces inflationary pressures and production timing uncertainties, with most new production benefits expected in fiscal 2027.
  • The company is repositioning the education business for sustainable growth, but short-term revenue is expected to remain flat.
  • Analysts noted the long sales cycles and uncertainties in state-sponsored programs.

Final Takeaway

Scholastic enters fiscal 2026 with reinforced confidence, targeting robust adjusted EBITDA growth of 20% at the midpoint and implementing significant cost-saving measures. Strategic integration across book publishing and distribution, capital returns to shareholders, and real estate monetization initiatives are expected to support further value creation, while the company remains focused on navigating persistent market pressures and unlocking the potential of its iconic IP portfolio.
